description: Turn Clicky from a server-proxied app into a shippable personal tool: each user installs the binary, opens a settings window on first launch, pastes their own API keys for Anthropic and/or z.ai (LLM) plus AssemblyAI (streaming transcription) and ElevenLabs (TTS), and Clicky talks directly to those providers from the user's machine. No Cloudflare Worker. No shared backend. No keys on disk in plaintext — all secrets encrypted with Windows DPAPI under the current user's profile. The existing worker/ directory remains in the repo because it is shared with the Mac reference implementation, but the Windows client no longer calls it in any code path. Users can switch between Claude and GLM at runtime via a tray menu without restarting. This PRD builds on the completed Windows port (see archive/prd-windows-port.json for the 17 shipped stories) and supersedes the earlier worker-routing GLM integration draft (see archive/prd-glm-worker-routing.json) that this project decided against because it required every user to run Cloudflare Wrangler locally. Target vision model for GLM: glm-4.6v. The Mac reference implementation is not modified.

userStories.title: ILlmClient interface + AnthropicDirectClient (direct to api.anthropic.com)
userStories.description: As a user with my own Anthropic API key, I want Clicky to call api.anthropic.com directly with my key instead of going through a worker, so I can run the app without any backend infrastructure.
acceptanceCriteria: New interface `Clicky.Api.ILlmClient` exposes `IAsyncEnumerable<string> SendAsync(IReadOnlyList<Message> history, IReadOnlyList<CapturedScreen> screens, string systemPrompt, string userText, CancellationToken ct)` — the exact surface `CompanionManager` already consumes, extracted from the existing `ClaudeClient`

The existing `Clicky.Api.ClaudeClient` is replaced by `Clicky.Api.AnthropicDirectClient : ILlmClient` that POSTs to `https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages` directly with headers `x-api-key: <userKey>`, `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01`, `content-type: application/json`
Constructor signature: `AnthropicDirectClient(string apiKey, string model = "claude-sonnet-4-6", HttpClient? httpClient = null)`. No proxy URL — the worker path is gone.

Request body shape, SSE parsing, PNG/JPEG magic-byte media_type detection, and TLS warmup HEAD request behavior are preserved byte-for-byte from the old ClaudeClient (see US-007 notes in archive/progress-windows-port.txt)

`Clicky.Companion.CompanionManager` now takes `ILlmClient` via constructor injection instead of constructing a `ClaudeClient` itself. App.xaml.cs constructs `AnthropicDirectClient` with a temporary hardcoded key for this story only — real key plumbing lands in US-019
All existing US-007 tests (SSE parsing, delta extraction, media type detection) pass against the new AnthropicDirectClient unchanged
One new test: a `FakeLlmClient : ILlmClient` verifies CompanionManager's dependency injection seam works end-to-end
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all existing Windows-port tests still pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-018 - ILlmClient interface + AnthropicDirectClient
userStories.notes: This is primarily a refactor + direct-API swap. Keep the scope narrow: don't add provider selection, key storage, or UI yet — those land in US-019 through US-024. The hardcoded temporary key is fine for this story because US-019 will replace it within one iteration.

userStories.title: SecretsStore (DPAPI) + SettingsStore (JSON) under %APPDATA%\Clicky
userStories.description: As a user, I want my API keys encrypted at rest using my Windows account credentials, and my non-secret settings persisted in a readable JSON file, so nobody can extract my keys from disk and I can inspect/reset my config without a GUI if needed.
acceptanceCriteria: `Clicky.Companion.SecretsStore` wraps `System.Security.Cryptography.ProtectedData.Protect` / `Unprotect` with `DataProtectionScope.CurrentUser`. Methods: `void Write(string key, string value)`, `string? Read(string key)`, `void Delete(string key)`, `bool Exists(string key)`
Secrets are stored as an encrypted blob at `%APPDATA%\Clicky\secrets.bin`. The plaintext internal format is a simple JSON dictionary; encryption is applied to the whole file, not per-entry

`Clicky.Companion.SettingsStore` reads/writes `%APPDATA%\Clicky\settings.json` with fields: `LlmProvider` (string: "anthropic" or "zai"), `LlmModel` (string), `ElevenLabsVoiceId` (string, default `kPzsL2i3teMYv0FxEYQ6` to match Mac), `OnboardingComplete` (bool), `AnalyticsOptOut` (bool)
Both stores auto-create the %APPDATA%\Clicky directory on first write
Keys used by SecretsStore are standardized constants: `anthropic_api_key`, `zai_api_key`, `assemblyai_api_key`, `elevenlabs_api_key`

App.xaml.cs on OnStartup instantiates SettingsStore + SecretsStore, reads the configured provider/model/keys, and passes them into the CompanionManager constructor (replacing the hardcoded key from US-018)

US-014's OnboardingService registry read is migrated: if `HKCU\Software\Clicky\onboarded` exists, copy its value to SettingsStore.OnboardingComplete on first run of this story's code, then prefer the SettingsStore value thereafter. Same for `analyticsOptOut`

Unit tests: SecretsStore round-trip (write → read → same value), SecretsStore decryption under a different user profile fails gracefully (returns null rather than throwing), SettingsStore round-trip, SettingsStore defaults when file is missing, migration from registry test
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass on a Windows test host
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-019 - SecretsStore (DPAPI) + SettingsStore under APPDATA
userStories.notes: DPAPI is per-user-account, not per-machine — backups restored to a different Windows user will fail to decrypt, which is correct security behavior. Document this in the SettingsWindow help text (lands in US-022). Do NOT add a passphrase fallback; the whole point of DPAPI is that it's invisible to the user.

userStories.title: ZaiDirectClient for GLM models (direct to api.z.ai OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
userStories.description: As a user with a z.ai API key, I want Clicky to use GLM-4.6V as a vision-capable alternative to Claude, with the same UI and screen-pointing behavior, so I have a choice of LLM providers.
acceptanceCriteria: New `Clicky.Api.ZaiDirectClient : ILlmClient` POSTs to `https://api.z.ai/api/paas/v4/chat/completions` with headers `Authorization: Bearer <userKey>`, `Content-Type: application/json`, `Accept: text/event-stream`
Constructor: `ZaiDirectClient(string apiKey, string model = "glm-4.6v", HttpClient? httpClient = null)`

Translates Clicky's existing Anthropic-shape inputs (system prompt + message history + CapturedScreen list + user text) into OpenAI chat-completions request body: system prompt becomes `messages[0] = {role:"system", content: systemPrompt}`, history turns pass through with their existing roles, the final user turn's text + screens become one user message with a `content` array of `{type:"text", text:...}` and `{type:"image_url", image_url:{url:"data:<media_type>;base64,<base64Data>"}}` entries
Image media_type is sniffed by PNG/JPEG magic bytes (reuse `AnthropicDirectClient.DetectImageMediaType` via refactor or duplicate — do not import a new library)

Streaming response parsing: reads `data: {...}` SSE lines, extracts `choices[0].delta.content` text chunks, yields them through the `IAsyncEnumerable<string>` return (same interface shape as AnthropicDirectClient). The `[DONE]` sentinel ends the stream cleanly
Chunk-boundary UTF-8 splits (a multi-byte character split across two HTTP chunks) do not corrupt the yielded text — use an incremental decoder, not a per-chunk Encoding.UTF8.GetString
Malformed JSON lines are logged and skipped, not thrown — one bad line must not kill the stream

Valid model values accepted: `glm-4.6v` (default, vision), `glm-4.5v` (fallback, vision). Text-only models (`glm-5.1`, `glm-5-turbo`, `glm-5`, `glm-4.5-air`, `glm-4.7-no-vision-variants`) must NOT be offered as choices because they silently break the [POINT:x,y:label:screenN] feature — Clicky requires vision

Unit tests: text-only request translation, single-image request translation, multi-image multi-screen request translation, SSE chunk parsing fixture (realistic glm-4.6v response with a [POINT:...] tag mid-stream), UTF-8 chunk-boundary split test, malformed-chunk skip test
End-to-end test with FakeHttpMessageHandler: canned OpenAI SSE input → expected string deltas out through ILlmClient.SendAsync
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-020 - ZaiDirectClient for GLM-4.6V
userStories.notes: This is the most algorithmically dense story. Budget time for the SSE chunk-boundary tests — getting them right is what separates a demo-quality integration from a shippable one. The Anthropic-shape system prompt (built by CompanionManager in US-010, includes the [POINT:x,y:label:screenN] tag instructions) passes through unchanged — GLM-4.6V is prompted in the same English, it just speaks OpenAI over the wire.

userStories.title: Direct-to-provider audio clients (AssemblyAI + ElevenLabs, no worker)
userStories.description: As a user with AssemblyAI and ElevenLabs API keys, I want Clicky to call those services directly with my keys, so I can run the app without any Cloudflare Worker.
acceptanceCriteria: `Clicky.Api.AssemblyAiStreamingTranscriber` is updated: the existing `FetchTokenAsync` that hits `{workerBaseUrl}/transcribe-token` is replaced with a direct call to `https://streaming.assemblyai.com/v3/token?expires_in_seconds=60` with header `Authorization: <userKey>` (AssemblyAI's token endpoint uses the raw API key, NOT `Bearer`)
Constructor: `AssemblyAiStreamingTranscriber(string apiKey, HttpClient? httpClient = null)` — proxyUrl parameter removed

The WebSocket connection to `wss://streaming.assemblyai.com/v3/ws` and all session mechanics (keyterms, sample_rate=16000, encoding=pcm_s16le, format_turns=true, speech_model=u3-rt-pro, partial/final turn processing, error callback, ForceEndpoint grace period) are preserved from US-008 unchanged

`Clicky.Api.ElevenLabsTtsClient` is updated: existing POST to `{workerBaseUrl}/tts` is replaced with a direct POST to `https://api.elevenlabs.io/v1/text-to-speech/{voiceId}/stream` with headers `xi-api-key: <userKey>`, `Content-Type: application/json`, `Accept: audio/mpeg` and body `{text, model_id: "eleven_flash_v2_5", voice_settings: {stability: 0.5, similarity_boost: 0.75}}`

Constructor: `ElevenLabsTtsClient(string apiKey, string voiceId, HttpClient? httpClient = null)`. The voice ID is now a constructor parameter (read from SettingsStore.ElevenLabsVoiceId by App.xaml.cs)
MP3 decoding via NAudio Mp3FileReader and WaveOutEvent playback path from US-009 is preserved unchanged

All existing US-008 and US-009 unit tests continue to pass after the refactor (session URI builder, keyterm serialization, partial/final dispatch, IsPlaying, StopPlayback, Dispose safety, cancellation)
New tests: direct token fetch URL + header format, direct TTS POST URL + header format, voice ID parameter threading
App.xaml.cs constructs both clients with keys + voice ID from SecretsStore + SettingsStore; CompanionManager receives them via constructor injection (matching the pattern from US-018/US-019)
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-021 - Direct AssemblyAI and ElevenLabs clients
userStories.notes: AssemblyAI's token-fetch auth uses the raw key in the Authorization header, not `Bearer <key>` — double-check this before coding. This is the last story that touches the Clicky.Api layer; after it, the worker is completely unused by the Windows client.

userStories.title: SettingsWindow WPF UI with Test buttons
userStories.description: As a first-time user, I want a friendly settings window where I can paste my API keys, pick my LLM provider and model, and verify each key works with a Test button before saving.
acceptanceCriteria: New `Clicky.App.SettingsWindow.xaml` — resizable, non-modal, dark theme matching CompanionPanelWindow, ~480×640 DIP default size, centered on primary display

Fields (top to bottom): **LLM Provider** (ComboBox: `Anthropic Claude`, `z.ai GLM`), **LLM Model** (ComboBox whose items change based on provider: Anthropic → `claude-sonnet-4-6`, `claude-haiku-4-5`, `claude-opus-4-6`; z.ai → `glm-4.6v`, `glm-4.5v`), **Anthropic API Key** (PasswordBox + Test button + `Get key →` link), **z.ai API Key** (PasswordBox + Test button + link), **AssemblyAI API Key** (PasswordBox + Test button + link), **ElevenLabs API Key** (PasswordBox + Test button + link), **ElevenLabs Voice ID** (TextBox, pre-filled with `kPzsL2i3teMYv0FxEYQ6`)

`Get key →` links open the default browser to: Anthropic → `https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys`, z.ai → `https://z.ai/manage-apikey/apikey-list`, AssemblyAI → `https://www.assemblyai.com/app/account`, ElevenLabs → `https://elevenlabs.io/app/settings/api-keys`

Each Test button fires a one-shot auth probe against the corresponding service using the currently-entered key (not the saved one), shows a spinner during the probe, and then turns green (✓) or red (✗) with a tooltip containing the error message. Probes: Anthropic → POST /v1/messages with `max_tokens: 1, model: claude-haiku-4-5, messages: [{role: user, content: "hi"}]`; z.ai → POST /api/paas/v4/chat/completions with `max_tokens: 1, model: glm-4.5v, messages: [{role: user, content: "hi"}]`; AssemblyAI → GET /v3/token?expires_in_seconds=60 (returns token on success, 401 on bad key); ElevenLabs → GET /v1/user/subscription (free auth check, returns subscription info)

Save button writes keys via SecretsStore, non-secret fields via SettingsStore, raises a `SettingsSaved` event, and closes the window. Save is enabled only when the required keys for the selected provider are entered (Anthropic provider → Anthropic key required; z.ai provider → z.ai key required; AssemblyAI + ElevenLabs keys always required because audio is mandatory)
Quit button (secondary, less prominent) calls Application.Current.Shutdown — required for the first-run case where the user doesn't want to enter keys
Help text at the bottom: small italic footnote explaining "Your keys are encrypted with your Windows account and stored only on this PC. Clicky never uploads them anywhere."
Window is reachable from the existing tray right-click menu via a new `Settings...` item (added between `Open` and `Quit`)
When reopened after initial save, fields are pre-populated from SecretsStore/SettingsStore (passwords shown as placeholder `••••••••` with a `Change` button that clears the field)
Unit tests: ViewModel command enablement (Save disabled until required fields entered), model list updates when provider changes, SecretsStore persistence on Save
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass. Manual: open Settings from tray, fill fields, all four Test buttons work against real services with a real key
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-022 - SettingsWindow UI with Test buttons
userStories.notes: Copy matters here — this is the onboarding experience for everyone who installs Clicky. Use friendly, plain English. Avoid words like 'provider', 'endpoint', 'API key rejected (401)'. Prefer 'Service', 'Couldn't reach Anthropic — check your key'. Do not add import/export or encryption passphrase features in this story; scope-creep risk is high.

userStories.title: First-run setup flow
userStories.description: As a first-time user, I want Clicky to walk me through entering my API keys the first time I launch it, and to re-open the settings window on subsequent launches if I'm missing a required key, so I never hit a confusing silent failure.
acceptanceCriteria: On App.OnStartup, after the existing SettingsStore + SecretsStore initialization (US-019), check: (a) are the currently-selected provider's LLM key and the two audio keys (AssemblyAI + ElevenLabs) all present in SecretsStore? (b) is SettingsStore.OnboardingComplete true?
If either check fails → open SettingsWindow (US-022) BEFORE the permissions panel from US-014 and block further app initialization until SettingsWindow raises SettingsSaved or the user quits
SettingsWindow in first-run mode shows a friendly header: "Welcome to Clicky — let's set up your API keys" instead of the normal "Settings" header

After successful save, SettingsStore.OnboardingComplete is set to true and the normal app init flow continues: construct tray icon → overlay manager → CompanionManager → permissions panel (US-014) auto-opens if any OS permission is still missing
On launches where both checks pass, the normal flow runs unchanged (tray icon appears silently in background, no window opens)
If the user closes SettingsWindow via the X button (not Save, not Quit) during first-run, the app exits cleanly — never leave the app half-initialized

Runtime detection: if the user deletes their key from SecretsStore externally (or DPAPI decryption fails due to a restored backup), the next push-to-talk attempt fails gracefully by surfacing the error to CompanionViewModel as a one-shot toast-style banner reading "Your API key is missing or invalid. Open Settings to fix.", AND re-opens SettingsWindow automatically

Unit tests: first-run with no secrets opens SettingsWindow, returning user with complete secrets does not, partial config (LLM key present but AssemblyAI missing) opens SettingsWindow with a highlighted inline error on the missing field
Manual test: delete `%APPDATA%\Clicky\secrets.bin`, launch app, verify SettingsWindow opens with welcome header
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-023 - First-run setup flow
userStories.notes: The failure-mode path (key deleted or revoked mid-session) is the one most likely to cause support issues for other users. Spend time on it: make the error surfacing specific, actionable, and non-modal. Do NOT silently retry.

userStories.title: Tray Model submenu for runtime LLM switching
userStories.description: As a user, I want to switch between Claude and GLM from the tray menu without restarting Clicky, so I can compare providers or hot-swap if one service is down.
acceptanceCriteria: TrayIconManager's context menu gains a new `Model` submenu inserted between `Settings...` and `Quit`
The submenu contains one radio-style item per available (provider, model) pair: Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Opus 4.6, GLM-4.6V, GLM-4.5V
The currently-active model is marked with ● (bullet) or a check-mark icon
Items whose required API key is NOT in SecretsStore are grayed out and show a tooltip: `Add your <provider> key in Settings to enable this model`

Clicking an enabled item triggers `CompanionManager.SwapLlmClientAsync(provider, model)`: (a) cancels any in-flight Claude/TTS task, (b) disposes the old ILlmClient, (c) constructs a new AnthropicDirectClient or ZaiDirectClient via the same factory App.xaml.cs uses at startup, (d) updates SettingsStore.LlmProvider + LlmModel, (e) updates CompanionViewModel.LlmModelDisplay so the panel footer reflects the change

CompanionManager.SwapLlmClientAsync is safe to call while the CompanionManager is in any VoiceState — if currently Listening/Processing/Responding, the swap waits for or cancels the current turn before taking effect, and the next push-to-talk uses the new client
ClickyAnalytics (US-016) fires a `model_switched` event with properties `{ from_provider, from_model, to_provider, to_model }` (respects the existing analyticsOptOut flag)
Unit tests: SwapLlmClientAsync transitions, grayed-out item enablement logic based on SecretsStore contents, VoiceState restoration to Idle after swap
Manual test: with a valid push-to-talk turn mid-stream, click a different model — verify the stream is cleanly cancelled and the next turn uses the new client
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-024 - Tray Model submenu for runtime LLM switching
userStories.notes: The mid-turn swap is the tricky case. Look at how US-010's CompanionManager already cancels currentResponseTask on re-press; SwapLlmClientAsync can reuse the same cancellation plumbing.

userStories.title: Shippable-product polish: README, installer notes, error copy, analytics events
userStories.description: As a prospective user finding Clicky on GitHub, I want a README that makes the value proposition clear, a setup flow I can complete without reading docs, and error messages that tell me how to fix problems rather than surfacing stack traces.
acceptanceCriteria: windows/README.md `Using Clicky` section rewritten for end-users (not developers): what it does, what keys you need, links to sign up for each service, approximate cost expectations, how to install
windows/README.md explicitly notes: `worker/` is legacy / optional shared-server mode used by the Mac reference implementation and not required for the Windows build

Top-level README.md (repo root) updated so the Windows port is presented as a first-class product, not an afterthought, with a screenshot of SettingsWindow and a screenshot of the blue cursor in action

All user-facing error copy audited: any `Exception.Message` directly surfaced to the UI must be replaced with a friendly `{what happened} + {what to do}` pair. Example: `Couldn't reach Anthropic — check your internet connection or your API key in Settings` instead of `The operation was canceled.`
CompanionViewModel exposes a new `LastError` string + clear command that the panel shows as a dismissable inline banner

ClickyAnalytics gets three new events: `onboarding_completed` (fired on first successful SettingsSaved), `settings_opened` (tray menu click), `provider_test_run` (properties: service, success, error_category). All respect analyticsOptOut

Installer script (windows/scripts/clicky-installer.iss from US-017) updated: the `[Files]` section ensures appsettings.json is NOT shipped (we no longer need WorkerBaseUrl there), uninstall section optionally offers to delete `%APPDATA%\Clicky\` contents with an explicit opt-in checkbox

Manual end-to-end test on a clean Windows 11 VM: run Setup_Clicky.exe, launch Clicky from Start Menu, complete SettingsWindow with real keys, grant permissions, perform one successful push-to-talk turn with Claude, switch to GLM via tray menu, perform one more successful turn. Document this in progress.txt
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-025 - Shippable polish for README installer copy analytics
userStories.notes: This is the story that distinguishes a hobby project from a product you can point friends at. Don't rush it. The copy audit is the most labor-intensive piece — grep for `throw new`, `.Message`, `MessageBox.Show`, and every user-visible string in XAML files.

userStories.title: Quit reliably terminates the process
userStories.description: As a user, when I click Quit from the tray menu I want the app to actually exit — no lingering Clicky.App.exe in Task Manager, no orphaned WASAPI / WebSocket / HttpClient threads keeping the process alive.
acceptanceCriteria: Reproduce the bug first: launch Clicky, do at least one push-to-talk turn (so mic capture + transcription WebSocket + LLM stream + TTS playback have all run), then click tray → Quit. Confirm Clicky.App.exe is still in Task Manager. Capture a managed-thread dump (procdump -ma, or debugger attach) BEFORE writing any fix so the culprit thread is identified, not guessed

Root-cause the hang. Likely suspects, in order: (a) `CompanionManager.Dispose` at `windows/src/Clicky.Companion/CompanionManager.cs:502-514` cancels `_hookConsumerCts` but never awaits the fire-and-forget `ConsumeHotkeyTransitionsAsync` task started at line 110; (b) `MicrophoneCapture.Dispose` at `windows/src/Clicky.Audio/MicrophoneCapture.cs:150-155` may not join the NAudio WASAPI thread; (c) HTTP calls inside `AnthropicDirectClient`, `ZaiDirectClient`, `ElevenLabsTtsClient`, `AssemblyAiStreamingTranscriber` may be running without a shutdown CancellationToken and blocking the dispatcher. Document in progress.txt which one(s) were actually holding the process alive

Fix the identified root cause(s). Required changes: store long-running background tasks as `Task` fields (not `_ = ...`) and `await` them with a 2-second timeout inside the owner's `Dispose`/`DisposeAsync`. Any new worker `Thread` must be `IsBackground = true`. Every outbound `HttpClient.SendAsync` / `ClientWebSocket.ReceiveAsync` on the shutdown path must accept a `CancellationToken` wired to a shutdown `CancellationTokenSource` owned by `App`

`App.xaml.cs` gains a shutdown `CancellationTokenSource` created in `OnStartup`, cancelled at the top of `OnExit` before any Dispose calls, and passed into CompanionManager/clients through their existing constructors (or a new `SetShutdownToken` method if adding a ctor param ripples too widely)

`App.OnExit` at `windows/src/Clicky.App/App.xaml.cs:427-459` is converted to dispose in a deterministic order: (1) cancel shutdown token, (2) await CompanionManager.DisposeAsync with 2s timeout, (3) dispose overlay manager, (4) dispose push-to-talk hook, (5) dispose tray icon. Add a final safety net: after `base.OnExit(e)`, if any foreground thread is still alive after 500ms, call `Environment.Exit(0)`. The safety net is belt-and-braces — it must NOT be used to paper over a hang the dispose chain should have prevented

Unit test: a `FakeLlmClient` and `FakeTranscriber` that intentionally block forever on an `HttpClient`-like stream. `CompanionManager.DisposeAsync` must still return within 2 seconds by cancelling them. Same pattern for `MicrophoneCapture`

Manual verification: after the fix, perform the original repro (one full push-to-talk turn, then Quit). Task Manager must show Clicky.App.exe gone within 1 second of the click. Repeat 3× — all three must terminate cleanly
Regression check: Quit from a cold state (app just started, no turn performed) must also work. Quit mid-turn (while TTS is speaking) must cancel playback AND exit within 1 second
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Append diagnosis + fix summary to progress.txt, including the exact thread stack(s) that were blocking shutdown
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-026 - Quit reliably terminates the process
userStories.notes: Diagnose before fixing. `Environment.Exit(0)` is a hammer that will hide bugs — fix the actual blocker first, then add the safety net. The `ShutdownMode="OnExplicitShutdown"` in App.xaml is correct for a tray app and must not be changed; any non-background foreground thread will prevent process exit under that mode, which is why hunting down the specific blocker matters.

userStories.title: Show Clicky version in tray menu + Settings window
userStories.description: As a user who just ran an auto-update, I want to see the current Clicky version in the tray and in Settings so I can verify the update actually installed without digging into Task Manager or the filesystem.
acceptanceCriteria: Add `<Version>1.0.0</Version>` to `windows/src/Clicky.App/Clicky.App.csproj` inside the existing `<PropertyGroup>`. This becomes the single source of truth that flows into `AssemblyInformationalVersion` at build time

New helper `Clicky.App.VersionInfo.Current` returns the version string via `Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly().GetCustomAttribute<AssemblyInformationalVersionAttribute>()?.InformationalVersion ?? "dev"`. Strip any `+<commithash>` suffix before returning
Tray icon tooltip is updated in `TrayIconManager.cs` where the `NotifyIcon` is constructed: set `Text = $"Clicky {VersionInfo.Current}"` so hovering the tray icon shows the version

Tray context menu gains a disabled info item `Version {x.y.z}` inserted just above the `Quit` item at `windows/src/Clicky.App/TrayIconManager.cs:89`. Use `IsEnabled = false` so it's visually greyed and non-clickable

Settings window shows the version at the bottom of the main grid in `windows/src/Clicky.App/SettingsWindow.xaml` as a small muted TextBlock (e.g. `Foreground="#888" FontSize="11"`) reading `Clicky {x.y.z}`. Place it alongside or just above the existing DPAPI help footnote

If `AutoUpdateService` is able to expose a `LatestKnownVersion` property without significant refactor, the Settings footer additionally reads `Clicky {current} — update available: {latest}` when latest > current. If exposing that is non-trivial, skip it for this story and leave a TODO — do NOT rewrite the auto-update plumbing
Unit test: `VersionInfo.Current` returns a non-empty string in a test run (validates the attribute is actually emitted by the csproj change)

Manual verification: build the installer, install it, hover the tray icon → tooltip shows `Clicky 1.0.0`. Right-click → menu shows `Version 1.0.0` greyed above Quit. Open Settings → footer shows `Clicky 1.0.0`. Bump the csproj version to 1.0.1, rebuild, reinstall → all three surfaces show 1.0.1
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-027 - Show version in tray and settings
userStories.notes: Tiny story on purpose — the goal is a reliable visual confirmation that updates landed, not a polished About dialog. Do not add an About window, changelog viewer, or update-check button in this story. Keep the diff under ~60 lines across 4 files.

userStories.title: Fix DPI-scaling bug that breaks pointer accuracy on high-DPI displays
userStories.description: As a user on a high-DPI Windows laptop, when I ask Clicky to point at something on screen I want the blue cursor to land on the actual target, not 20–50% off. On scaled displays (125%, 150%, 200%) the pointer currently lands far from the intended element, and the error grows with distance from the monitor origin.
acceptanceCriteria: Reproduce the bug on a 150% DPI display first. Ask Clicky to point at a well-known target (Windows taskbar clock, a specific file in the VS Code explorer). Document the pixel offset and its scaling behavior in progress.txt before fixing

Root cause: physical pixels are being handed to WPF APIs that expect DIPs. Three files are implicated: `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:425-437` returns monitor bounds from `GetMonitorInfo` in physical pixels; `windows/src/Clicky.Pointing/PointTagParser.cs:94-103` produces a `screenPoint` in physical pixels by scaling and offsetting with those bounds; `windows/src/Clicky.Overlay/OverlayWindow.cs:41-48` assigns those physical-pixel values directly to WPF `Window.Left/Top/Width/Height` which are always DIPs (the comment on line 41-43 asserting otherwise is wrong); `windows/src/Clicky.Overlay/BlueCursorControl.cs:197-198` then subtracts physical-pixel overlay bounds from a physical-pixel target to compute canvas-local coordinates, which WPF also interprets as DIPs

Fix strategy: keep screenshot→physical-pixel math in the capture/parser layer, and convert to DIPs exactly once at the WPF boundary. Introduce a `DpiHelper` (or similar) that calls `GetDpiForMonitor(MDT_EFFECTIVE_DPI)` per `HMONITOR` and returns a `(double ScaleX, double ScaleY)` tuple, plus a convenience `System.Windows.Point ToDips(System.Drawing.Point physical, HMONITOR monitor)`

`OverlayWindow` constructor converts `monitorBounds` from physical pixels to DIPs before assigning `Left/Top/Width/Height`. Store both the original physical `MonitorBounds` (for matching against PointDirective.DisplayBounds) AND the new DIP bounds (for layout). The comment on line 41-43 is deleted and replaced with one that correctly describes WPF's DIP coordinate system

`BlueCursorControl.FlyTo` converts `targetPoint` and the `GetCursorPos` result from physical pixels to DIPs before computing canvas-local coordinates. Uses the overlay's cached DPI scale to avoid re-querying the OS on every animation tick

`PointTagParser.ConvertToScreenCoordinates` is NOT changed — it continues to return physical-pixel global coordinates. The DIP conversion happens at the overlay-layer boundary, giving a single conversion point that future code can trust

Unit test: `DpiHelper` round-trip (physical → DIP → physical) is identity for a monitor at 100%, and scales correctly for 125% / 150% / 200%. Provide a fake monitor-DPI source so tests don't depend on the real display config
Unit test: `PointTagParser` output is unchanged (no regression in the existing physical-pixel contract)
Unit test: a test `OverlayWindow` (or extracted helper) given a physical-pixel target on a 150% monitor produces a DIP canvas-local point matching the expected value within 0.5 DIP

Manual verification on a high-DPI laptop: hotkey → "point at the Windows clock" → the blue cursor must land on the clock, not beside it. Repeat on the far corner of the screen (where the DPI bug causes the largest offset) — must still land within ~20 px of the target. If multi-monitor is available, test the same on a secondary monitor at a different DPI
Regression check on a 100% single-monitor setup: accuracy must be unchanged from before the fix
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Append the reproduced offset values (before) and the post-fix measurements (after) to progress.txt
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-028 - Fix DPI scaling for pointer accuracy on high-DPI displays
userStories.notes: The secondary hypothesis — that the image dimensions sent to Claude (AnthropicDirectClient.cs:63-76) disagree with the resized JPEG dimensions — should only be investigated if DPI alone doesn't close the gap. Measure before and after. Do NOT change the coordinate contract of PointTagParser; keeping it in physical pixels means future code (e.g. a hypothetical Win32 SendInput click feature) still has the right reference frame.

userStories.title: Stream TTS + overlap capture to cut perceived latency
userStories.description: As a user, I want Clicky to start speaking almost as soon as I let go of the push-to-talk key, not after several seconds of silence. Today, TTS only starts playing after the whole ElevenLabs MP3 has been downloaded AND the entire LLM response has streamed — both are serialized pointlessly.
acceptanceCriteria: Measure current latency as a baseline before changing anything. Instrument `CompanionManager.SendTranscriptToClaudeWithScreenshot` at `windows/src/Clicky.Companion/CompanionManager.cs:318-396` to log (a) key-release→first-LLM-token, (b) first-LLM-token→first-TTS-byte, (c) first-TTS-byte→first-audio-heard. Record the baseline numbers in progress.txt

Change 1 — stream TTS audio instead of buffering the whole MP3. Update `windows/src/Clicky.Api/ElevenLabsTtsClient.cs:62-92` to consume the HTTP response as a `Stream` and feed it into NAudio via a buffered pipe (`MediaFoundationReader` over a `BufferedWaveProvider`, or a custom `Stream` wrapper that blocks `Read` until more bytes arrive). First audio sample must leave the speakers no later than ~300 ms after the first HTTP response byte arrives, not after the full MP3 is downloaded

Change 2 — start TTS on sentence boundaries instead of waiting for the whole LLM response. In `CompanionManager` where the LLM `IAsyncEnumerable<string>` is consumed, buffer tokens until the first sentence terminator (`.`, `!`, `?`, or two consecutive newlines), kick off an ElevenLabs TTS request for that chunk, continue buffering the next sentence, and queue subsequent TTS requests to play sequentially without gaps. The `[POINT:x,y:label:screenN]` tag must still be stripped before the text is spoken (`PointTagParser.Parse` already handles this — call it per-sentence)

Change 3 — overlap screen capture with the hotkey press. Today capture runs after key release. Kick off `ScreenCapture.CaptureAllScreensAsJpegAsync` when the key is first pressed (inside the hotkey press handler in `CompanionManager`), store the `Task<List<CapturedScreen>>`, and `await` it at the point the LLM request is built. On key release the capture is usually already done, saving 300–1000 ms. Cancel the capture task if the press is shorter than ~100 ms (accidental tap)

Change 4 — tighten the WGC first-frame timeout at `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:171` from 2 seconds to 800 ms. On Windows 11, WGC first-frame is normally <300 ms; the 2 s ceiling just prolongs failure fallback to GDI+

Change 5 (optional, only if time permits) — pre-warm HTTPS connections to `api.anthropic.com`, `api.z.ai`, `api.elevenlabs.io`, and `streaming.assemblyai.com` on `App.OnStartup` with a throwaway HEAD or GET. Skip with a TODO if it adds complexity

Cancellation: every new async path (pipelined TTS queue, capture-on-press, streamed audio reader) must honor `CompanionManager`'s existing cancellation plumbing from US-010. A new push-to-talk must cleanly cancel any in-flight TTS playback and queued sentences, matching the existing behavior for full-response TTS

Ordering guarantee: sentences must play in the order they were emitted by the LLM. Test with a mocked LLM that emits sentences at varying speeds and a mocked TTS client with variable latency — the audio queue must never reorder

Unit tests: sentence-boundary tokenizer (handles `[POINT:...]` tag stripped mid-sentence, handles newlines, handles abbreviations like `Dr.` or `e.g.` gracefully — accept some false positives but document them), streaming MP3 reader starts playback before stream completes, pipelined TTS queue preserves order under varying latency, capture-on-press cancellation on short tap

Manual measurement after the fix: the same three latency metrics from the baseline, with each change applied incrementally. Target: end-to-end key-release→first-audio-heard under 1.5 seconds on a typical broadband connection (baseline today is likely 3–6 s). Document actual before/after numbers in progress.txt
Regression check: pointing accuracy from US-028 must be unchanged — the capture-on-press change must still hand Claude the correct screenshot dimensions and `CapturedScreen.DisplayBounds`
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-029 - Stream TTS and overlap capture for perceived latency
userStories.notes: Ship changes 1+2+3 as the core of this story — they compound. Change 4 is a trivial one-line win. Change 5 is optional. Sentence-boundary detection is a known tar pit; accept a simple `.!?` + newline heuristic, do not pull in a sentence-tokenizer library, and do not attempt to handle edge cases like `Dr. Smith` perfectly — if a false split causes a short extra TTS call, that is acceptable. Measure, don't guess: the baseline and post-fix numbers in progress.txt are the acceptance gate, not code review aesthetics.

userStories.title: Higher-resolution screenshots for better pointer accuracy on all displays
userStories.description: As a user on any display resolution, I want Clicky to send higher-resolution screenshots to the LLM so that the [POINT:x,y] coordinates it returns are more precise, and the blue cursor lands closer to the intended UI element.
acceptanceCriteria: Increase `MaxDimension` from 1280 to 2048 in `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:20`. This halves the coordinate error on 2560px displays and reduces it by 3× on 4K (3840px) displays — every pixel of LLM imprecision now maps to fewer physical pixels

Lower `JpegQuality` from 80 to 75 in `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:21` to partially offset the larger image payload. At 2048px the JPEG is about 40-60% larger at quality 75 vs 1280px at quality 80, which is acceptable for broadband connections and stays well within Claude and GLM-4.6V's image size limits

Replace `(int)` truncation with `(int)Math.Round(...)` in `CalculateScaledSize` at `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:377-378`. Current code `(int)(MaxDimension / aspect)` truncates fractional pixels — on ultrawide displays (e.g. 3440x1440, aspect 2.388) this causes a 1-pixel dimension mismatch between the reported `ScreenshotPixelWidth/Height` and the actual JPEG, which accumulates into a pointing offset at screen edges

Fix off-by-one clamping in `PointTagParser.ConvertToScreenCoordinates` at `windows/src/Clicky.Pointing/PointTagParser.cs:89-90`. Change `Math.Min(directive.X, target.ScreenshotPixelWidth)` to `Math.Min(directive.X, target.ScreenshotPixelWidth - 1)` and same for Y — coordinates are 0-indexed so the max valid coordinate is `dimension - 1`, not `dimension`
Update any test fixtures that assert `MaxDimension == 1280` to expect 2048. Grep for `1280` in `windows/tests/` to find them

These changes must improve accuracy on ALL display configurations, not just high-DPI: (a) standard 1920×1080 at 100% — screenshot is 1920×1080 (no resize needed, within 2048), improving precision from 1.5:1 to 1:1 compared to the old 1280px cap; (b) 2560×1440 at 100% — screenshot is 2048×1152 (1.25:1 instead of 2:1); (c) 3840×2160 at 150% — physical capture is 3840×2160, resized to 2048×1152 (1.875:1 instead of 3:1); (d) 3440×1440 ultrawide — resized to 2048×857 (1.68:1 instead of 2.69:1)

Regression: existing US-028 DPI tests and US-029 capture-on-press tests must still pass. The `ScreenshotPixelWidth/Height` metadata stored in `CapturedScreen` must still match the actual JPEG dimensions exactly — verify with a unit test that captures a known-size bitmap and checks the metadata
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-030 - Higher-resolution screenshots for pointer accuracy
userStories.notes: This is the single highest-impact change for pointing accuracy. At 1280px, Claude is routinely off by 10-20 screenshot pixels, which maps to 20-60 physical pixels depending on the display. At 2048px the same 10-20 pixel error maps to 12-37 physical pixels — a 40-50% improvement with zero changes to the LLM prompting. The trade-off is slightly larger API payloads (~150KB vs ~100KB per screenshot), which adds 50-100ms on broadband but is negligible compared to the 500-2000ms LLM TTFT. Do NOT go above 2048 — larger images don't measurably improve vision model coordinate accuracy but do increase cost and latency.

userStories.title: Eager first-chunk TTS to cut time-to-first-audio
userStories.description: As a user, I want to hear the beginning of Clicky's response as fast as possible after I release the push-to-talk key, instead of waiting for the LLM to produce a complete sentence ending with a period before TTS starts.
acceptanceCriteria: Add an eager first-chunk mode to `SentenceTokenizer` at `windows/src/Clicky.Companion/SentenceTokenizer.cs`. Add a private `bool _firstChunkEmitted = false` field. When `_firstChunkEmitted` is false and the buffer has accumulated at least 60 characters, `FindSentenceBoundary` additionally considers `,` (comma), `—` (em-dash, U+2014), `–` (en-dash, U+2013), `;` (semicolon), and `:` (colon) followed by a space as valid split points, in addition to the existing `.!?\n\n` boundaries

After the first chunk is emitted via the eager path, set `_firstChunkEmitted = true` and revert to the standard `.!?\n\n` sentence-boundary logic for all subsequent chunks — this prevents choppy playback after the initial response
The 60-character minimum prevents pathological splits on very short opening phrases like `yeah,` or `sure,` — these should buffer until a normal sentence boundary or the eager threshold is hit

Example: Claude streams `you'll want to open the color inspector — it's right up in the top right area of the toolbar.` The old tokenizer waits for the final `.` (entire sentence). The new tokenizer emits `you'll want to open the color inspector` at the em-dash after ~45 tokens, then `it's right up in the top right area of the toolbar.` at the period. Estimated savings: 500-1500ms off first-audio latency depending on LLM token rate
Edge case: if the first `.!?` arrives before the buffer hits 60 chars, the normal sentence boundary fires first and `_firstChunkEmitted` is set — no double-split risk
Edge case: [POINT:...] tags must not be counted toward the 60-char threshold. Strip point tags from the length check (not from the actual text — stripping happens downstream in CompanionManager)

Unit tests: (a) eager split on comma after 60+ chars, (b) eager split on em-dash, (c) no eager split when buffer < 60 chars (waits for normal boundary), (d) normal `.!?` boundary still fires if it comes before 60 chars, (e) second sentence uses normal boundaries only (no mid-comma splits), (f) `[POINT:...]` tag not counted in threshold
This improvement works identically on all hardware — it is pure text-processing logic with no OS, DPI, or device dependencies
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-031 - Eager first-chunk TTS splitting
userStories.notes: This pairs with US-032 (streaming MP3 playback). Together they cut 800-2300ms from first-audio latency. Alone, this saves 500-1500ms because the TTS request fires earlier. Do NOT reduce the 60-char threshold below 50 — very short TTS chunks sound robotic and are wasteful (ElevenLabs has per-request overhead). Do NOT add clause-level splitting for sentences after the first — it makes the voice choppy. The first sentence is the only one where latency matters more than naturalness.

userStories.title: True streaming MP3 playback for TTS
userStories.description: As a user, I want Clicky to start playing the TTS audio as soon as the first MP3 frames arrive from ElevenLabs, instead of waiting for the entire MP3 file to download before playback begins.
acceptanceCriteria: Rewrite `PlayStreamingMp3Async` in `windows/src/Clicky.Api/ElevenLabsTtsClient.cs:98-157`. Currently the method downloads the entire MP3 into a `MemoryStream`, then creates an `Mp3FileReader` and plays. The new implementation must start playback after the first few MP3 frames are decoded, while the HTTP download continues in parallel

Implementation approach: (a) create a `BufferedWaveProvider` at 44100 Hz stereo (or whatever format the first `Mp3Frame` reports) with a buffer of ~5 seconds; (b) start `WaveOutEvent`/`WasapiOut` playing from the `BufferedWaveProvider` immediately; (c) in a loop, read chunks from the HTTP response stream, decode MP3 frames using `Mp3Frame.LoadFromStream` + `IMp3FrameDecompressor` (NAudio's `AcmMp3FrameDecompressor`), and add decoded PCM to the `BufferedWaveProvider`; (d) when the HTTP stream ends, wait for the `BufferedWaveProvider` to drain (playback completes)

The `BufferedWaveProvider.ReadFully` property must be set to `true` so it returns silence instead of stalling when the HTTP stream is temporarily slower than playback — this prevents audio glitches on slow connections
Handle format detection: the first `Mp3Frame` determines the sample rate and channel count. Create the `BufferedWaveProvider` and `IMp3FrameDecompressor` after the first frame is decoded, not before

Preserve the existing `StopPlayback()` semantics: calling `StopPlayback()` mid-stream must (a) stop the `WaveOutEvent`/`WasapiOut`, (b) cancel the HTTP download loop, and (c) dispose all resources. Use the existing `_lock` for thread safety
Preserve the existing `IsPlaying` property: return true while the `WaveOutEvent` is in `PlaybackState.Playing`

Preserve the WasapiOut path for specific output devices (`_outputDeviceId`): the `CreateWavePlayer` helper is unchanged, the only difference is what `IWaveProvider` is passed to `Init()` — `BufferedWaveProvider` instead of `Mp3FileReader`

Estimated latency savings: 300-800ms per TTS sentence. The old path waited for the full MP3 download (typically 300-800ms for a 1-2 sentence chunk at ElevenLabs' output rate). The new path starts playback after ~2-3 MP3 frames (~50-100ms of audio data)

This works on all Windows machines — NAudio's `Mp3Frame` and `AcmMp3FrameDecompressor` use the ACM codec that ships with every Windows installation since Vista. No additional codec or library is required

Unit test: mock HTTP stream that feeds MP3 data in 1KB chunks with 50ms delays. Verify `IsPlaying` becomes true before all chunks are delivered (proving playback started during download). Use a real short MP3 fixture (generate one with NAudio or include a ~1s test file)
Cancellation test: start streaming playback, cancel after 200ms — verify no resource leaks (all disposed within 1s)
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-032 - True streaming MP3 playback for TTS
userStories.notes: The current `PlayStreamingMp3Async` already has the scaffolding for streaming (the `playbackStarted` flag, the `startPlaybackThreshold` constant) but the comment at line 126-128 says 'For simplicity we'll wait for the full download since NAudio's Mp3FileReader needs seekable streams.' The fix is to bypass `Mp3FileReader` entirely and decode frame-by-frame with `Mp3Frame` + `AcmMp3FrameDecompressor` into a `BufferedWaveProvider`. This is a well-documented NAudio pattern. Do NOT pull in MediaFoundation or any external codec — the built-in ACM MP3 decoder is universally available and sufficient.

userStories.title: TLS warmup for ElevenLabs to eliminate first-request handshake delay
userStories.description: As a user, I want the first TTS request to ElevenLabs to be as fast as subsequent ones, by pre-establishing the TLS connection at app startup instead of paying the handshake cost on the first push-to-talk turn.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a `WarmUpTlsConnectionIfNeeded` method to `ElevenLabsTtsClient` at `windows/src/Clicky.Api/ElevenLabsTtsClient.cs`, following the exact same pattern as `AnthropicDirectClient.WarmUpTlsConnectionIfNeeded` at `windows/src/Clicky.Api/AnthropicDirectClient.cs:182-204`
Use a static lock + static bool flag (`_hasStartedTlsWarmup`) to ensure the warmup HEAD request fires exactly once across all `ElevenLabsTtsClient` instances, same as the Anthropic pattern

The warmup fires `Task.Run(async () => { HEAD https://api.elevenlabs.io })` on construction. The HEAD request uses the existing `_httpClient` instance so the connection pool is shared with subsequent TTS requests. Set `ConnectionClose = false` to keep the connection alive
Failures are silently swallowed (try/catch around the whole warmup) — this is purely an optimization, not a requirement. A failed warmup just means the first TTS call pays the full TLS handshake
Estimated savings: 100-300ms on the first TTS request. Subsequent requests reuse the pooled connection regardless
This works on all networks and all Windows configurations — it's a standard HTTP optimization with no OS-specific dependencies
Unit test: construct `ElevenLabsTtsClient` with a `FakeHttpMessageHandler`, verify that a HEAD request to `https://api.elevenlabs.io` is made within 1 second of construction
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-033 - TLS warmup for ElevenLabs TTS
userStories.notes: Smallest story in this batch — should be under 30 lines of code. The Anthropic client already does this (US-018 preserved it from the original ClaudeClient). Copy the pattern exactly. Do NOT add warmup for AssemblyAI — the transcriber's WebSocket connection is established per-session and the token-fetch HTTP call is lightweight enough that the TLS handshake doesn't dominate.

userStories.title: Version bump to 1.1.0 for accuracy + latency improvements
userStories.description: As a user, I want the version number to reflect that meaningful improvements to pointer accuracy and response latency have shipped, so I can confirm I'm running the updated build.
acceptanceCriteria: Bump `<Version>` from `1.0.0` to `1.1.0` in `windows/src/Clicky.App/Clicky.App.csproj`
Verify that `VersionInfo.Current` (from US-027) returns `1.1.0` — run the existing unit test or add a quick assertion if one doesn't exist
Verify tray tooltip shows `Clicky 1.1.0` (hover the tray icon), tray context menu shows `Version 1.1.0` (right-click), and Settings window footer shows `Clicky 1.1.0`
If the Inno Setup installer script at `windows/scripts/clicky-installer.iss` has a hardcoded version string, update it to `1.1.0` as well
This story must only be committed AFTER US-030, US-031, US-032, and US-033 have all passed — it is the final seal on the accuracy + latency improvement batch
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: chore: US-034 - Bump version to 1.1.0
userStories.notes: Tiny story — a single line change in the csproj plus verification. The version bump signals to users (especially those using auto-update from US-017) that the accuracy and latency improvements from US-030 through US-033 have landed. Do not bump to 2.0.0 — these are enhancements, not breaking changes.
